data.title: Green Brick Partners (GRBK) Margin Compression Reinforces Cautious Narratives Heading Into Q1 2026
data.description:
Green Brick Partners (GRBK) has just opened Q1 2026 reporting season with a recent run of quarterly results that show total revenue between US$497.6 million and US$567.3 million and basic EPS ranging from US$1.67 to US$2.32 over the last six reported quarters, including net income between US$74.3 million and US$103.1 million. Over the past year, the company has seen trailing twelve month revenue around US$2.1 billion and basic EPS in a tight band between US$7.09 and US$8.51. This gives you a clear sense of how the top line and per share earnings have been holding up ahead of this latest update, while shifts in net margins sit at the center of how the market is likely to read the new numbers.

See our full analysis for Green Brick Partners.

With the recent figures on the table, the next step is to line them up against the most common narratives around Green Brick Partners and see which views the latest margins and earnings support and which ones get put to the test.

See what the community is saying about Green Brick Partners

Advertisement

Margins Ease Back From 18% To 14.8%

  • Over the last 12 months, Green Brick’s net profit margin sat at 14.8%, compared with 18% a year earlier, while trailing twelve month net income was US$310.4 million on about US$2.1b of revenue.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative flags pressure from higher price concessions and incentives, and the margin figures line up with that concern, as:
    • Trailing net margin moving from 18% to 14.8% fits with the idea that richer incentives are eating into profitability even though revenue over the last reported twelve months stayed around US$2.1b.
    • At the same time, the company still reported US$310.4 million of trailing net income, which shows earnings remain solidly positive even as consensus worries about margin compression.

Trailing P/E Of 9.4x Versus 15x Peers

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 9.4x, compared with about 15x for peers and 11.8x for the wider US Consumer Durables group. The current share price of US$67.44 also sits below a DCF fair value of about US$98.74.
  • Consensus narrative points to valuation as a key potential reward, and the data here clearly supports that angle, because:
    • The 9.4x P/E sits at a discount to both peers and the broader industry, which fits the view that the stock is priced more conservatively than many competitors despite trailing twelve month net income of US$310.4 million.
    • The share price being about 31.7% under the DCF fair value of US$98.74, yet above the analyst price target of US$62.00, shows how different valuation tools are giving mixed signals that readers need to weigh for themselves.

Five Year Growth Record Versus Latest Slowdown

  • Over the past five years, earnings grew at an average rate of 16% per year, while the most recent year showed negative earnings growth alongside trailing margin easing from 18% to 14.8%.
  • Consensus narrative highlights tension between longer term earnings growth and more recent softness, and the reported figures underline that push and pull, because:
    • The 16% annual earnings growth over five years sits next to a year of declining earnings, which is consistent with commentary that record activity in key markets like Texas and Atlanta coexists with pressure from affordability and incentives.
    • Trailing twelve month EPS of US$7.09 to US$8.51 over recent quarters lines up with that story of strong past growth now rubbing up against headwinds that could affect how durable those earlier earnings gains prove to be.

Next Steps

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Veradermics (MANE) After A 162% Year To Date Surge?
data.description:
• Wondering if Veradermics at around US$99 a share still lines up with the story you have in mind, or if the price is already baking in a lot of optimism.
  • The stock has seen sharp recent moves, with returns of 43% over the last 7 days, 56.8% over the last 30 days, and 162.3% year to date. This can change how you think about both risk and potential reward.
  • Recent headlines around Veradermics have focused on its position in the pharmaceuticals space and investor interest in its prospects, helping to frame these strong short term returns. This context matters because sentiment shifts can influence pricing just as much as fundamentals.
  • Even with this backdrop, Veradermics currently scores 2 out of 6 on our valuation checks, as shown by its valuation score. Next up is a closer look at how different valuation methods stack up and why there may be an even better way to judge fair value by the end of this article.

Veradermics scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Veradermics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what a stream of future cash flows is worth right now.

For Veradermics, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $71.63 million. Analyst and model projections then move through a period of negative free cash flows, such as $69 million in 2026 and $95.50 million in 2027, before reaching a projected free cash flow of $123 million in 2030. Beyond the analyst window, Simply Wall St extrapolates cash flows out to 2035 using its own growth assumptions.

Combining all of these discounted cash flows results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $250.72 per share. Against a current share price around $99, the DCF output implies the stock trades at roughly a 60.5% discount to this estimate, which indicates a wide gap between price and modeled value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Veradermics is undervalued by 60.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 51 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Veradermics.

Approach 2: Veradermics Price vs Book

For companies in pharmaceuticals, price to book, or P/B, can be a useful yardstick because it compares what you pay in the market with the accounting value of net assets on the balance sheet. For profitable, established businesses, investors often look for a P/B that reflects both the quality of those assets and the earnings they can support.

Growth expectations and risk also shape what feels like a normal P/B level. Faster growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty can point to a lower one. This is why simple comparisons can be tricky.

Veradermics currently trades on a P/B of 30.09x, which sits well above the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 2.63x and the peer group average of 4.33x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what P/B might make sense given Veradermics’ earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risks. Because it pulls these company specific factors together, the Fair Ratio can be a more tailored benchmark than broad industry or peer averages.

In this case, Veradermics’ actual P/B of 30.09x is meaningfully higher than the Fair Ratio. This points to the shares screening as expensive on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/B ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 18 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Veradermics Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Meet Narratives, a simple way to put a clear story behind your numbers by linking your view of a company to its forecast revenue, earnings, margins and ultimately a fair value estimate.

A Narrative connects three pieces in one place: the company story you believe, the financial forecast that story implies and the fair value you arrive at. This allows you to quickly compare that value to the current share price and decide whether the gap looks appealing or not.

Narratives are available on Simply Wall St’s Community page, used by millions of investors, and they update automatically when new information such as news, filings or earnings is added. This helps ensure your view does not stay frozen while the facts move.

For Veradermics, one investor might build a Narrative that points to a high fair value based on optimistic revenue growth and improving margins, while another might set a much lower fair value with more cautious revenue assumptions and modest profitability. Both of those views are visible and comparable against today’s Veradermics share price.

Do you think there's more to the story for Veradermics? Head over to our Community to see what others are saying!

data.title: ANZ Group Holdings (ASX:ANZ) Margin Compression Puts Bullish Narratives To The Test
data.description:
ANZ Group Holdings (ASX:ANZ) has put fresh numbers on the table for H1 2026, with total revenue for the most recent half year at A$10.7 billion and basic EPS at A$0.76, set against trailing 12 month revenue of A$21.5 billion and EPS of A$1.98. Over recent halves, the bank has seen revenue move from A$10.1 billion in H2 2024 to A$11.0 billion in H1 2025 and A$10.7 billion in H2 2025, while basic EPS shifted from A$1.04 to A$1.23 and then A$0.76. This gives a clear view of how the top line and per share earnings have tracked into this result. With net profit margin over the last year at 27.4% versus 32.1% the year before, the focus now turns to how investors weigh that margin pressure against the bank's forward growth profile.

See our full analysis for ANZ Group Holdings.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing bull, bear and consensus narratives that have been building around ANZ over the past year.

See what the community is saying about ANZ Group Holdings

Advertisement

Margin pressure and costs in focus

  • Over the trailing 12 months, ANZ’s net profit margin is 27.4% compared with 32.1% the year before, while the cost to income ratio over the last reported period is 58.8% versus 52.2% in the prior half year where it was disclosed.
  • Consensus narrative expects efficiency gains from the Suncorp Bank acquisition and the shift to a lower cost dual platform to support margins, yet the recent rise in the cost to income ratio and the current margin level give a clear reference point for how much operating leverage those initiatives would need to offset.
    • Supporters of the bullish view point to projected synergies from Suncorp and the planned decommissioning of legacy systems from 2026 to 2028 as potential cost savings drivers, while the move from a cost to income ratio of 52.2% to 58.8% in the latest disclosed periods shows that technology and integration spending are currently weighing on efficiency.
    • At the same time, consensus commentary highlights expectations for improved net margins over time as digital and AI driven platforms scale, which sits against the reported step down in net profit margin from 32.1% to 27.4% and gives investors a concrete benchmark to test whether those future margin goals are being met.

Bulls argue that the real test for these margin numbers will be how quickly ANZ converts its platform plans and Suncorp integration into tangible cost savings and profitability gains, and that is exactly what the detailed bull case narrative unpacks in more depth. 🐂 ANZ Group Holdings Bull Case

Loan book growth and credit quality

  • Total loans reported in recent halves sit at A$803.3b in H2 2024, A$820.2b in H1 2025 and A$829.5b in H2 2025, while non performing loans over those same periods are A$1.7b, A$2.3b and A$2.5b respectively.
  • Consensus narrative frames the Suncorp acquisition and focus on Queensland as a way to build scale in lending, and these loan and non performing loan figures give a useful check on that story by showing both growing exposure and the level of stressed loans attached to it.
    • Supportive readers of the bullish case point to expected larger and earlier synergies from Suncorp as a growth driver for the loan book, and the step up from A$803.3b to A$829.5b across the last three reported halves illustrates that ANZ is already operating with a very large lending base that could benefit from any efficiency gains.
    • On the cautious side, bears focus on credit risk and regulatory scrutiny, and the movement in non performing loans from A$1.7b to A$2.5b over the same sequence of halves offers a concrete series for assessing how credit quality trends against any future macro or regulatory changes mentioned in the risk list.

Mixed signals from growth and valuation

  • Earnings are forecast to grow about 9.6% per year with revenue at about 4.5% per year, while ANZ trades on a trailing P/E of 18.2x versus domestic peers at 18.6x and the Global Banks industry at 10.9x, and a DCF fair value of A$40.80 compares with a current share price of A$35.61 and an analyst consensus price target of A$36.34.
  • Analysts’ consensus view links expected earnings growth and digital initiatives to that valuation mix, so the combination of a P/E slightly below peers, a premium to the wider banking group and a share price that sits below both the DCF fair value and the consensus target creates a clear set of checkpoints for how the story is priced.
    • Supporters of the bullish angle highlight that the DCF fair value of A$40.80 is around A$5 above the current A$35.61 share price and that earnings forecasts of about 9.6% per year sit alongside high assessed earnings quality, which some investors may see as support for the case that the current price leaves room if those forecasts are met.
    • More cautious readers point out that ANZ’s 18.2x P/E is still materially higher than the Global Banks average of 10.9x and that dividend history is flagged as unstable, which aligns with the idea in the consensus narrative that higher technology and regulatory costs or slower than expected growth could make that premium harder to justify.

If you want to see how different investors connect these earnings, forecasts and valuation checks into a full story, it is worth reading the detailed community narrative that pulls the numbers together in one place. 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about ANZ Group Holdings.

Next Steps

With this mix of risks and rewards on the table, the key question is how it all stacks up for you right now. Take a closer look at the data, weigh it against your own expectations, and use the full breakdown of 2 key rewards and 1 important warning sign.

See What Else Is Out There

For investors watching TSX:TRI, this court approval confirms that a previously announced corporate action is moving closer to execution. Over a longer horizon, the shares show a 20.6% gain over five years, with returns over three years at a 15.6% decline.

The approved plan will directly affect existing holders through the return of capital and a change in share count once the consolidation takes effect. A special cash distribution returns funds to shareholders, while the reverse stock split reduces the number of shares and raises the trading price per share without changing the company’s total equity value. For you as an investor, the economic impact will depend on tax treatment of the capital return in your market and how your broker handles fractional shares following the consolidation. The transactions are also a signal about how management currently views excess cash versus reinvestment or acquisitions.
